content.full_text:
California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
February 26, L99O
Iris P. Yang
McDonough, Holland & Allen
555 capitoL Uall, Suite 95o
Sacramento, cA 9 5814
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. A-90-067
Dear Ms. Yang:
You are seeking advj.ce on behalf of the city of Half lloon Bay
and of Mr. David Iverson regarding his duties and responsibi lities
under the confl ict-of-interest provisions of the Political Refonn
Act (the rraqgn1.1 As !!r. Iverson's authorized representative, we
provide you with advice pursuant to Regulation 18329(b).
The followi,ng advice is based upon the facts provided in let-
ters from you on January 23, L99O, and from !lr. Iverson on
January f9, 1990, as well as facts provided in your telephone
conversations with this agency on January 17, L99o, and in l{r.
Iverson,s telephone conversations with this agency on January 17,
1990 and February 22, L99O.
OUESTION
!1r. Iverson olrns a home within a residential area that is to
be included in a proposed redevelopuent project area. can he
participate in decisions on the adoption of the proposed redevel-
opnent plan?
CONCLUS ION
!lr. Iverson Day not participate in the city council,s deci-
sion to adopt the proposed redevelopnent pIan.
1 Gorr"rrrr"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the covernDent Code unless otherwise indicated. Comrnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations section
18000, et seo. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vision 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P,O. Box 8O? . Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322-5660


File No. A-90-067
Page 2
FACTS
David Iverson is a member of the city council of the City of
Half ltoon Bay and in that capacity also sits as a nember of the
city,s redevelopment agency. The city has initiated proceedings
to adopt a redevelopnent p1an, the rrNorth wavecrest Redevelopment
Project,rr lrhich includes the entire dorrntorrn area and tlro small
residential neighborhoods. The residential neighborhoods cornprise
approxinrately 10 to 15 percent of the cj,ty's residential area and
no nore than 10 percent of the city's population.
The plan is expected to benefit the two residential areas
through public infrastructure inprovements, as well as by various
types of rehabilitation and improvement assistance to residential
property owners in the two areas.
ttr. Iverson owns his place of residence within one of the two
residential areas to be included in the proposed redevelopment
plan. His property is unique: it includes an historical house,
built in 1906, and constructed of reinforced concrete, an unusual
construction. when built, the house was the first to be located
in the particular section of the city, and is the on).y such his-
toric horne in the imnediate area of the trro residential areas.
ANALYSI S
The Act prohibits a public official fron uraking, participat-
ing in, or usingT his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a finan-
cial interest. 
(Section 87100. )
A public official 
has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a nate-
rial financial effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the pub-
1ic aenerally, on the official or a nember of his immediate fam-
i Iy. (section 87103. )
Mr. Iverson is a public official 
because he is an elected
nember of the HaIf ltoon Bay City Council and also sits as a member
of the city's redevelopment agency. (Section A2O4A. ) 
Therefore,
l,!r. Iverson nust disgualify hirnself fron any city counci-I decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable uaterial financial effect
on hin or on his real property interest that is distinguishabte
from the public generally.
Soreseeable l,taterial FLaancial, Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. While certainty
is not required, an effect that is uerely a possibility is not -


FiIe No. A-90-067
Page 3
reasonably foreseeable. (Dolrnev Cares v. Downev Communitv Deve-
lopment Com. (1987) 196 Ca1.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC ops. 198. )
IVe have previously advised that the anticipated result of a
redevelopDent p1an, whose purpose is the proBotion of sound de-
velopment and the j,mprovement of blighted areas, is an increase in
property values and an improved business clinate 
( Bro$rn Advice
Letter I-89-547, copy enclosed.) Thus it i.s reasonably foresee-
able that the decisions regarding the redevelopnent plan will have
a financial effect on property located within the redevelopment
area .
In order to deterrnine whether the foreseeable effect of a
decision is uraterial, the coDmission has adopted Regulations 18702
et seq. 
In pertinent part, Regulation La7O2.1 (copy enclosed)
provides that the effect of a decision is naterial when:
***
(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the
prelininary plan, to forn a project area committee,
to certify the environmental docuuent,
to adopt the redevelopnent p1an, to add territory
to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend
any of the above decisions,' and real property in
which the official 
has an interest, or any part of
it is located within the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the redevelopnent area.
(Regulation La7 02 .1 ( a) (3) (D)
Because Mr. Iverson owns property within the boundaries or pro-
posed boundaries of the redevelopment area, the financial effect
of a decision to adopt the redevelopment plan will be deened nate-
rial. 
Therefore, !1r. Iverson is precluded fron participating in
the council's decision to adopt the plan if the effect is distin-
guishable from the effect on the public generally (section
871o3. )
Tb€ llPublic ceneral,lyrt Erceptlon
The financial effect of the council,s decision on }tr. Iverson
is distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally unless
the decision will affect Mr. Iverson in substantially the sane
Danner as it will affect all members of the public or a signifi-
cant seginent of the public. (Requlation 18703; In re Leqan (1985)
9 FPPC Ops. 1,' In re Oiren (197 6') 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copies enclosed.)
When the agency in question is an elected body, the "public
generallyrr is comprised of the entire jurisdiction of the agency


File No. A-9O-067
Page 4
in guestion, as all of the residents are constituents of the elec-
ted members. Because you have indicated that the project area for
the proposed redevelopment plan includes only portions of the city
(the entire downtown comrnercial area and two small residential
neighborhoods), it is clear that the impact of the proposed rede-
velopuent plan will affect some seqnents of the city differently
fron others.
Regulation 18703 also pernits the application of the "public
generallytt exception when a decision affects the official's 
in-
terests in substantially the same Danner as it will affect a sig-
nificant segTroent of the public. 
The financial effect of the
forthcoroing decision would appear to affect Mr. Iverson's property
in substantially the same nanner as it will affect other homeown-
ers in the two small residential neighborhoods. The question then
rernaining is nhether home owners in these neighborhoods constitute
a significant segment of the city's residents. (In re Lecran,
supra . )
A group that is large in nunber and heterogeneous in quality
can constitute a significant segTnent of the public for the pur-
poses of the public generally exception. (fn re Ferraro (1978) 4
i'rnc op.. 62, copy eiclosea.j2 Heie, however, the only group af-
fected by the council's decision in the saDe manner as !lr. Iverson
would be the home owners in the two residential neighborhoods.
The facts given indicate that the proposed project area represents
betueen 10 percent and 15 percent of the city's existing
resj.dential area, and no nore than 10 percent of the city's
population.
Without a greater proportion of the city's residents residing
or otherwise concentrated within these two neighborhoods, the
homeohrners in the two neighborhoods do not constitute a signifi-
cant segment of the public in the city. 
For this reason, and be-
cause of the uniqueness of !Ir. Iverson's property itself, 
vre are
unable to conclude that the council,s decision on the redevelop-
ment plan will affect Ur. Iverson in the saDe uanner as the public
generally, The rrpublic generally[ exception therefore does not
apply in this situation.
2 In.re owen, In re Lesan, and In re Brown (Lg78) 4 FppC Ops. 19)
explain the application of the public Aenerally concept. Also
enclosed for your infornation are the follording advice tetters:
wes! Advice Letter, A-89-243, Scher Advice Letter A-88-479, Fl.vnn
Advice Letter, I-8 8-4 3 O.


Fi■e No. A-90-067
・           Page 5
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further guestions regarding this rnat-
ter please contact ne at (916) 322-509]-.
Sincerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
/&€lhb
By: / Jonathan S. Rothman
/ 
counsel, Legal Division
KED:」SR:plh
Enclosures
